select committees over the time the process

There are a number of ways that could be done.
B not dispensing with select committee scrutiny ever,
and improving select committee scrutiny in ways that are,
allow the committees to go into more depth.
Now, I know that there is one big reason

This parliament, the House of Representatives is very small.
You take the executive out of it.
The number of mps that are available for scrutiny
is quite small,
and you've got 12 subject select committees

And the result is that you have a very uneven workload.
And, and I think measures have to be

the lack, the absence
of good technical scrutiny of legislation

Um, excellent. Just three quick points from me, Mr. Speaker.
Uh, the first would be regarding the high volume
of public submissions.
Uh, here I would probably beg
to differ a little bit with, to Jeffrey.
Um, I think there is a case where you have hundreds
of thousands of submissions
for having a more selective process, in which,
as I've set out in my submission, you could have a range of,
of possibilities, um, which could be combined.
One would be mps on

So I think there's ways forward, which would avoid some of

to sit through ever, uh, just very large numbers

for discussion and debate.
Uh, I think that's a terrible waste

Speaker, and I think you have it in your power in this

There's two other things, just very briefly.
It's unlikely we're going to be able to increase the number

But Parliament can increase its advisory capacity, uh,
as the, uh, staff from the o office
of the Auditor General were highlighting earlier today.
Um, their capacity to contribute to parliament's process

I think has been in incredibly helpful, uh, for democracy

But we lack some of the resources

We, we don't have a parliamentary budget office,
for example, and we don't have a parliamentary science
advisor, a chief science advisor.
Many departments have chief science advisors.
Parliament has nothing in the science area.
And there are very few scientists in Parliament.
That really worries me actually,
because so many of the issues that you folk have to grapple
with have a strong science background.
And you are often very heavily dependent on, uh,
a limited number of, um, advisors, uh, uh,
often people working for the executive.
So increasing the, the advisory capacity of Parliament,
